conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility properly, I'll need to ask you a few questions.

First, let me review the official eligibility rules so I can guide you accurately.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now I can help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit.

Let me start with the first question:

**Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'm looking to claim Child Benefit for my child. I have one child - Alex, who is 8 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. So you're inquiring about Child Benefit for Alex, who is 8 years old.

Now I need to ask: **Do you live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. Now I need to understand your relationship with Alex.

**Do you live with Alex, or are you paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or the equivalent in kind) towards looking after them - for example on food, clothes or pocket money?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex doesn't live with me, but I do contribute towards their upkeep - I help with things like clothes, food, and pocket money.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. This is an important point - since Alex doesn't live with you, I need to understand the arrangement better.

**Where does Alex currently live, and how much do you contribute towards their upkeep each week or month?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex lives with my ex-partner. I contribute about £26 a week towards their upkeep - that covers things like clothes, school supplies, and helping out with food and other bits they need.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that detail. That's helpful to know.

Now I need to ask: **Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex - specifically, is your ex-partner claiming it?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, my ex-partner isn't claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Nobody else is getting it for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. I have a few more questions to ensure I have a complete picture.

**Is Alex in hospital, in care, or in any residential accommodation provided by the local council?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in hospital or care or anything like that. They're just living at home with my ex-partner.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Just a couple more questions to complete my assessment.

**Is Alex currently working 24 hours or more a week, or receiving any benefits in their own right (such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nothing like that - Alex is only 8, so they're not working and they don't get any benefits themselves.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you for confirming that. One final question:

**Do you or your partner (if you have one) have an individual income over £60,000 per year?**

(This won't affect your eligibility, but it's important to know as there may be a tax charge to consider.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of us earns over £60,000.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for answering all my questions. I now have enough information to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Let me review the rules carefully:

Based on the official guidance, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK. You're usually considered responsible for a child if either:
- You live with them, OR
- You're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or the equivalent in kind) towards looking after them

In your situation:
- Alex is 8 years old (under 16) ✓
- You live in the UK ✓
- You don't live with Alex, but you contribute £26 per week towards their upkeep
- Nobody else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex ✓
- Alex is not in hospital, care, or working ✓

The current Child Benefit rate for the eldest or only child is £25.60 per week. Since you're contributing £26 per week towards Alex's upkeep (which includes clothes, school supplies, food, and other necessities), this meets the threshold of paying "at least the same amount as Child Benefit" towards their care.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

You meet all the eligibility requirements because:
- Alex is under 16 and you live in the UK
- Even though Alex doesn't live with you, you're considered responsible for them because you're contributing £26 per week towards their upkeep, which is more than the current Child Benefit rate of £25.60 per week
- No one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex

You can proceed with making a claim for Child Benefit. Since neither you nor your partner earns over £60,000, you won't need to worry about the High Income Child Benefit tax charge.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), and the claimant lives in the UK. Although Alex doesn't live with the claimant, the claimant is responsible for Alex by contributing £26 per week towards their upkeep (covering clothes, school supplies, food, and other necessities). This exceeds the Child Benefit rate of £25.60 per week for an eldest or only child, meeting the requirement of paying 'at least the same amount as Child Benefit' towards looking after them. No one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and Alex is not in hospital, care, working, or receiving benefits in their own right. Therefore, the claimant qualifies for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 8-year-old child Alex. Despite not living with Alex, the claimant contributes £26 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which exceeds the Child Benefit threshold of £25.60 per week, establishing them as responsible for the child. All other eligibility criteria are met, and no one else is claiming for Alex.
